section.title: Joshua's Farewell
section.sectionRationale: Old and near death, Joshua gathers Israel's leaders for a farewell charge. He reminds them that the LORD fought for them and gave the land, and he presses one thing above all: hold fast to the LORD, love Him, obey His law, and do not turn to the gods of the nations. He warns that the same God who kept every good promise will bring disaster if they break the covenant. It is one movement, Joshua's farewell to the leaders, a solemn call to covenant faithfulness at the end of a faithful life.
section.thesis: Because the LORD faithfully fought for Israel and kept every promise, Joshua's dying charge is that they cling to Him alone in love and obedience, refusing the gods of the nations, for the God who blessed them will just as surely judge them if they forsake the covenant; His faithfulness cuts both ways.
section.frameNote: Three beats: the LORD has fought for you and will drive out the nations, verses 1 to 5; hold fast to the LORD and do not turn to other gods, verses 6 to 13; and God kept every promise, and will as surely judge covenant-breaking, verses 14 to 16.

units.label: The LORD has fought for you
units.note: it was the LORD your God who fought for you

verses.text: A long time after the LORD had given Israel rest from all the enemies around them, when Joshua was old and well along in years,
he summoned all Israel, including its elders, leaders, judges, and officers. “I am old and well along in years,” he said,
“and you have seen everything that the LORD your God has done to all these nations for your sake, because it was the LORD your God who fought for you.
See, I have allotted as an inheritance to your tribes these remaining nations, including all the nations I have already cut off, from the Jordan westward to the Great Sea.
The LORD your God will push them out of your way and drive them out before you, so that you can take possession of their land, as the LORD your God promised you.

units.label: Hold fast to the LORD and do not turn away
units.note: hold fast to the LORD your God; love Him

verses.text: Be very strong, then, so that you can keep and obey all that is written in the Book of the Law of Moses, not turning aside from it to the right or to the left.
So you are not to associate with these nations that remain among you. You must not call on the names of their gods or swear by them, and you must not serve them or bow down to them.
Instead, you shall {{davaq|hold fast}} to the LORD your God, as you have done to this day.
The LORD has driven out great and powerful nations before you, and to this day no one can stand against you.
One of you can put a thousand to flight, because the LORD your God fights for you, just as He promised.
Therefore watch yourselves carefully, that you {{ahav|love}} the LORD your God.
For if you turn away and cling to the rest of these nations that remain among you, and if you intermarry and associate with them,

know for sure that the LORD your God will no longer drive out these nations before you. Instead, they will become for you a snare and a trap, a scourge in your sides and thorns in your eyes, until you perish from this good land that the LORD your God has given you.

units.label: God kept every promise, and will as surely judge
units.note: not one promise has failed; likewise the calamity will come

verses.text: Now behold, today I am going the way of all the earth, and you know with all your heart and soul that not one of the good {{dabar|promises}} the LORD your God made to you has failed. Everything was fulfilled for you; not one promise has failed.

But just as every good thing the LORD your God promised you has come to pass, likewise the LORD will bring upon you the calamity He has threatened, until He has destroyed you from this good land He has given you.

If you transgress the covenant of the LORD your God, which He commanded you, and go and serve other gods and bow down to them, then the anger of the LORD will burn against you, and you will quickly perish from this good land He has given you.”

davaq.gloss: To cling, hold fast, stick to, be joined to. The same word describes a man being joined to his wife, Genesis 2:24, and it is the heart of Joshua's charge: hold fast to the LORD your God. It calls for a clinging, covenant-loyal devotion to God alone, the opposite of clinging to the nations and their gods, verse 12, which uses the same verb.

ahav.gloss: To love. Joshua charges Israel to watch themselves carefully that they love the LORD their God. Covenant faithfulness is not cold rule-keeping but love; the same call stands at the heart of the law, love the LORD your God with all your heart, Deuteronomy 6:5, and Jesus names it the greatest commandment. Obedience flows from love for God.

dabar.gloss: A word, promise, matter. Joshua declares that not one good dabar of all the LORD promised has failed. The same reliability of God's word that made the good promises come true will make the threatened calamity come true if Israel breaks covenant; God's word is faithful in blessing and in warning alike, so it must be heeded.

basic.context: Years have passed since the conquest, and Joshua is now very old. Like Moses before him, he gathers Israel's leaders to give a farewell charge. His message is simple and urgent. First, remember: it was the LORD who fought for you and gave you this land; He will finish driving out the remaining nations. Second, therefore hold fast to the LORD, love Him, obey His law, and above all do not mix with the surrounding nations or worship their gods. Third, a sober warning: just as God kept every single good promise He made, so He will just as certainly bring the disaster He warned of if Israel breaks the covenant and turns to idols. Joshua stakes everything on God's faithfulness, which blesses the obedient and judges the unfaithful. The question underneath is: how do God's people stay faithful once the great victories are past and the pressure of comfort and compromise sets in?
basic.hermeneutics: This is historical narrative, a covenant farewell speech in the pattern of Deuteronomy. Its warnings about intermarriage and the nations are covenant-specific to Israel, aimed at guarding pure worship of the LORD, not a general rule about ethnicity; the danger was spiritual, being drawn into idolatry. The enduring lessons are the call to wholehearted love and loyalty to God, the danger of compromise with the surrounding culture's gods, and the truth that God's faithfulness cuts both ways, blessing and judgment.
basic.originalLanguages: Joshua's central charge is to davaq, to hold fast, to cling, to the LORD, and to ahav, to love, Him. And he grounds it in the reliability of God's dabar, His word: not one good promise failed, so the warned calamity will just as surely come if they break covenant.
basic.typology: Joshua's charge to hold fast and love the LORD points to the greater Joshua's summary of the law, to love God with all one's heart. And the warning that covenant-breaking brings judgment, later fulfilled in exile, points to the need for a Savior who keeps the covenant on His people's behalf and writes God's law on their hearts.
basic.christ: Joshua, near death, calls Israel to cling to the LORD, but Israel's later history shows they could not keep the covenant, and the threatened calamity of exile came. This drives us to Christ, who perfectly loved and obeyed the Father, kept the covenant for us, bore the curse of covenant-breaking, and gives His people a new heart to love and hold fast to God.
basic.god: God is the faithful warrior who fought for Israel and kept every promise. He is worthy of exclusive, loving devotion, jealous for His people's whole-hearted loyalty. And He is just: the same faithfulness that brought every blessing will bring judgment if His people forsake Him. His character is consistent in mercy and in justice.
basic.crossReferences: Deuteronomy 6 commands loving the LORD with all one's heart. Deuteronomy 28 lays out the blessings and curses of the covenant. Matthew 22 names love for God the greatest commandment. And Jeremiah 31 promises a new covenant with the law written on the heart.
basic.oneStory: At the end of his life, Joshua charges Israel to hold fast to the LORD and love Him, grounding the call in God's perfect faithfulness, which blesses the obedient and judges the unfaithful. The warning, later fulfilled in exile, points beyond itself to Christ, who keeps the covenant His people could not and writes God's law on their hearts so they can truly cling to Him.

context.literary: Chapter 23 is Joshua's first of two farewell addresses, spoken to the leaders, paralleling Moses' farewell in Deuteronomy. It is structured around the contrast between God's proven faithfulness and the danger of Israel's future unfaithfulness, moving from remembrance to exhortation to warning. The refrain that not one of God's good promises failed, verse 14, echoes the summary of 21:45, binding the book together. The speech sets up the fuller covenant renewal at Shechem in chapter 24.
context.historical: Joshua speaks after a long period of rest, likely near the end of a life that reached 110 years, 24:29. The remaining nations, still to be driven out, posed a constant temptation to intermarriage and idolatry, which is exactly what would ensnare Israel in the period of the Judges, Judges 2:11-13. The warning about the nations becoming a snare, a scourge in the sides, and thorns in the eyes uses vivid covenant-curse imagery. The concern with intermarriage was religious: foreign spouses would draw Israel to foreign gods, as later happened even to Solomon.
context.canonical: The speech stands in the Deuteronomic tradition of covenant blessing and curse, Deuteronomy 28 to 30, and its warning proved tragically accurate: Israel's turning to idols led ultimately to exile from the good land, just as Joshua foretold. Its call to love and hold fast to the LORD is the heart of the law that Jesus names the greatest commandment, Matthew 22:37-38. And the reality that Israel could not keep this covenant points forward to the new covenant, in which God writes His law on the heart and gives His people the will to cling to Him, Jeremiah 31:33 and Ezekiel 36:26-27.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To record Joshua's farewell charge to Israel's leaders, calling them to remember God's faithfulness, to hold fast to the LORD in love and obedience, to refuse the gods of the nations, and to heed the warning that the God who kept every promise will judge covenant-breaking as surely as He blessed covenant-keeping.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The specific warnings about the Canaanite nations and intermarriage address Israel's covenant situation and the spiritual danger of idolatry, not a rule about ethnicity for all time. What endures is the call to wholehearted love and loyalty to God, the warning against compromise with idolatry, and the truth that God is faithful both to bless and to judge, so His covenant must be taken seriously.

debates.question: Why does Joshua so strongly warn against associating and intermarrying with the nations?

views.label: The danger was spiritual, not ethnic
views.case: The concern throughout is that the nations and their gods would draw Israel into idolatry, verse 7; the prohibition on intermarriage is aimed at the worship of other gods that foreign alliances brought, exactly the ruin that later overtook Solomon, so the issue is guarding pure worship of the LORD, not racial purity, and outsiders who embraced the LORD, like Rahab, were welcomed.

views.label: A guard for covenant identity in a critical era
views.case: At the vulnerable moment of settlement, surrounded by the very peoples under judgment for their idolatry, Israel needed a clear boundary to preserve its distinct covenant loyalty; the strong warning fenced the young nation from absorbing the practices that would dissolve its devotion to the LORD, a boundary tied to that redemptive-historical moment.

debates.bearing: Both readings agree the aim was to keep Israel faithful to the LORD alone; the warning targets idolatry and spiritual compromise, and it calls God's people in every age to guard whole-hearted devotion against the pull of the surrounding culture's gods.

study.originalLanguages: Joshua's charge turns on two verbs of the heart and one word about God's word. The first is davaq, to hold fast, to cling; you shall hold fast to the LORD your God, verse 8. It is the word for a husband cleaving to his wife, Genesis 2:24, and it pictures a covenant loyalty that sticks close to God and refuses every rival; tellingly, the same verb is used in the warning, if you cling instead to the nations, verse 12, so the choice is which one Israel will hold fast to. The second is ahav, to love; watch yourselves carefully that you love the LORD your God, verse 11. Faithfulness here is not cold duty but love, the very heart of the law that Jesus calls the greatest commandment. And undergirding it all is God's dabar, His word: not one good word of all He promised has failed, verse 14, and precisely because His word is that reliable, the calamity He warned of will just as surely come if Israel breaks covenant. Cling, love, and heed His faithful word: the vocabulary makes covenant loyalty a matter of the whole heart, resting on a God whose every word comes true.

typology.type: The call to hold fast to and love the LORD alone
typology.fulfillment: The greatest commandment, fulfilled by Christ who loved the Father perfectly and enables His people to love God, Matthew 22:37-38 and 1 John 4:19.

typology.type: The warning that covenant-breaking brings judgment and exile
typology.fulfillment: The curse of the broken law borne by Christ, who redeems His people from it, Galatians 3:13 and Deuteronomy 28:15-68.

typology.type: A covenant Israel could not keep
typology.fulfillment: The new covenant in which God writes His law on the heart and gives His people the will to cling to Him, Jeremiah 31:31-34 and Ezekiel 36:26-27.

study.christ: Joshua's farewell rings with a call Israel could not finally answer. He charges them to hold fast to the LORD, to love Him, to refuse the gods of the nations, and he warns that the God who kept every promise will bring the threatened calamity if they break covenant. Israel's later history is the tragic proof that they could not keep it: they clung to other gods, and the exile Joshua foresaw came upon them. This drives us to Christ. Where Israel, and we, fail to hold fast and love God as we ought, Jesus loved the Father perfectly and kept the covenant flawlessly on His people's behalf. And the curse Joshua warned of, the calamity of the broken covenant, fell not on those who trust Him but on Christ Himself, who redeemed us from the curse of the law by becoming a curse for us, Galatians 3:13. More than that, He inaugurates the new covenant, in which God writes His law on the heart and gives a new spirit, so that His people are enabled at last to cling to Him in love, Jeremiah 31:33 and Ezekiel 36:26-27. Joshua could command love; Christ creates it.
study.god: This chapter reveals a God who is faithful, worthy of exclusive love, and just. He is the faithful warrior who fought for Israel, drove out great nations, and gave them rest and land, so that not one of His good promises failed. He is worthy of whole-hearted, clinging love; He will not share His people's devotion with the gods of the nations, and He calls them to hold fast to Him alone. And He is just: His faithfulness is not sentimentality but integrity, so the same God who kept every good word will bring the calamity He warned of if His people forsake the covenant. Joshua wants Israel to grasp that God's blessing and God's judgment flow from the same faithful character; to presume on His grace while breaking His covenant is folly. This God is to be loved, clung to, and taken with utter seriousness, and, in Christ, He becomes the God who enables the very faithfulness He demands.

crossReferences.note: Love the LORD your God with all your heart, the command at the center of Joshua's charge.
Not one of God's good promises failed; Joshua repeats this to ground both his call and his warning.
After Joshua, Israel served the Baals, the very unfaithfulness he warned against, leading to the cycle of the Judges.
Christ redeemed us from the curse of the law by becoming a curse for us, bearing the calamity Joshua warned of.
In the new covenant God writes His law on the heart, enabling the faithful love Joshua could only command.

study.oneStory: Near the end of his life, Joshua charges Israel's leaders to hold fast to the LORD and love Him, grounding the call in God's flawless faithfulness, which blesses the obedient and just as surely judges the unfaithful. His warning would come true in Israel's later idolatry and exile, exposing a covenant they could not keep and pointing beyond themselves to Christ, who loved and obeyed perfectly, bore the covenant curse, and by the new covenant writes God's law on His people's hearts so they can truly cling to Him.

quiz.q: What did Joshua remind Israel was the reason for their victories?

options.text: That it was the LORD their God who fought for them.
options.note: Verses 3 and 10 credit all the victories to the LORD fighting for Israel.

options.text: That their army was superior to the nations.
options.note: Joshua credits the LORD, not Israel's military strength, for the victories.

options.text: That the nations were weak and disorganized.
options.note: The nations were great and powerful; the victory came because the LORD fought for Israel.

options.text: That Joshua's own leadership won the wars.
options.note: Joshua points to the LORD, not himself, as the one who fought for them.

quiz.q: What was the heart of Joshua's charge to Israel?

options.text: To hold fast to the LORD and love Him, refusing the gods of the nations.
options.note: Verses 6 to 11 call Israel to cling to and love God alone.

options.text: To expand their territory by any means necessary.
options.note: God would drive out the nations, but the charge was loyal love to God, not territorial ambition.

options.text: To build a grand temple immediately.
options.note: The charge was covenant faithfulness, not temple-building, which came generations later.

options.text: To make alliances with the surrounding nations.
options.note: The opposite: he warned them not to associate with the nations or their gods.

quiz.q: How does Joshua use God's faithfulness in his warning?

options.text: As God kept every good promise, so He will surely bring the threatened calamity if they break the covenant.
options.note: Verses 14 to 16 show God's faithfulness cuts both ways, in blessing and in judgment.

options.text: He argues that God's faithfulness means they can never face judgment.
options.note: The opposite: the same faithfulness that blessed will also judge covenant-breaking.

options.text: He says God's promises might or might not come true.
options.note: Joshua stresses not one promise failed; God's word is certain, in blessing and warning alike.

options.text: He claims God's warnings are only empty threats.
options.note: Joshua treats the warnings as certain to be fulfilled, just as the promises were.

quiz.q: How does Joshua's charge point beyond itself to Christ?

options.text: Israel could not keep the covenant, so it points to Christ, who kept it for us, bore its curse, and writes God's law on our hearts.
options.note: The unkept covenant drives us to Christ and the new covenant, Galatians 3:13 and Jeremiah 31:33.

options.text: It proves people can keep God's covenant perfectly by trying harder.
options.note: Israel's later failure shows they could not; the answer is Christ, not mere effort.

options.text: It shows God's law does not really matter.
options.note: God's law matters deeply; the point is that Christ fulfills it and enables obedience.

options.text: It has no connection to Christ.
options.note: The unkept covenant and the promised calamity point clearly to Christ, who keeps the covenant and bears its curse.

questions: Joshua urged Israel to hold fast to the LORD once the great victories were behind them. Where are you most tempted to drift from God in seasons of comfort and ease?
The charge was to love God, not merely to obey rules. Would you describe your walk with God more as loving devotion or as cold duty, and what would deepen your love for Him?
Joshua warned against being drawn into the worship and ways of the surrounding culture. Which of the culture's gods, success, comfort, approval, most tempts your loyalty away from God?
God's faithfulness means both His promises and His warnings come true. How does taking God's warnings as seriously as His promises change the way you live?

Joshua could command love and loyalty, but only Christ can create them by writing God's law on the heart. How does resting in Christ, who kept the covenant for you, free and fuel your own faithfulness?

translationNotes.note: Hold fast renders davaq, to cling or be joined; the same verb appears in verse 12 for clinging to the nations, sharpening the contrast between loyalty to God and to idols.
A snare, a scourge in your sides, and thorns in your eyes is vivid covenant-curse imagery for the trouble the un-driven-out nations would bring, fulfilled in Judges.
Going the way of all the earth is a dignified way of speaking of approaching death; the phrase frames Joshua's words as a farewell testament, like Moses' in Deuteronomy.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for Joshua 23:1-16. Prose farewell speech. No em or en dashes present in the source or introduced; none anywhere in the file. Sermon left as none; no sermon URL was verified this session.
